w01: Right. This is the PROSPER walkthrough… the whole system, start to finish, in about twenty-five minutes. By the end you'll know exactly what you're looking for on a chart, what makes a trade live… and what kills one. Quick ground rule before we start. This system makes one decision a day, in the evening, on closed candles. That's it. No staring at screens. No lunch-break peeking. One look… one decision… done. Use this walkthrough with your thumb on pause. When I draw on the chart, stop and really look. If you remember one thing from this slide: the goal isn't to trade more. It's to trade correctly, about once or twice a week.
w02: Let's deal with the thing most traders treat as a weakness… your time. You've got the school run. A day job. Real life. So when can you actually trade? Be honest. About half nine in the evening, once the house is quiet. Here's the twist… that constraint is the edge. Evening trading forces you onto closed four-hour and daily candles. Closed candles can't lie to you. Day traders drown in noise and spreads… you simply aren't there for any of it. Your orders are pending orders, placed once, with the stop and target attached. The market can do what it likes while you sleep. If you remember one thing: you're not trading around your life. The life schedule is what makes the system mechanical.
w03: So what's the actual edge? One idea, over a century old… trends persist. When gold is properly trending, the next move is more likely to continue than to reverse. Academics call it time-series momentum. They've tested it back to the eighteen-eighties, across every major market. It's one of the only edges retail traders can genuinely harvest… because it doesn't need speed, or news, or a feel for the market. It needs patience and rules. PROSPER is a trend-continuation system. We never guess tops. We never catch falling knives. We wait for a trend… wait for it to pause… and join it when it resumes. That's the whole trick. If you remember one thing: we don't predict. We follow… and we only follow when five specific conditions line up.
w04: Five gates. C1 to C5. Every evening, you walk them in order… and the answer is binary at each step. Here's the logic. C1, C2, C3 are the structural gates… direction, location, momentum. If any one of those fails… the idea is DEAD tonight. Not maybe. Dead. Close the laptop. C4 and C5 are the timing gates… the trigger candle, and the news window. If structure passes but timing fails… that's WAIT. The setup is alive, you check again tomorrow evening. And only when all five tick… is it GO. Five out of five. There is no four-and-a-half. Say the three words out loud now… dead… wait… go. That's your entire vocabulary as a trader. If you remember one thing: three of the five gates failing doesn't mean a smaller trade. It means no trade.
w05: Gate one. Direction. Look at this daily gold chart. See the blue line flowing through the candles? That's the fifty-day E M A… the system's compass. The rule. Where did today's daily candle close? Below the line… you are only allowed to short. Above it… longs only. Simple. But there's a refinement. Near the line, price just chops… so we add a no-trade band, half an A T R either side. Close inside that band? The market hasn't picked a side… C1 fails, DEAD, done in ten seconds. Look at this chart again. Price under the line for weeks… one rally back up to touch it… rejected… trend resumes. That rally into the line is exactly where shorts get paid. If you remember one thing: C1 doesn't find trades. It deletes half the mistakes you'd otherwise make.
w06: Gate two. Location. A trend without a pullback is a trade you've already missed… chasing is how accounts bleed. So C2 asks one question. Has price pulled back into the zone? Watch the chart… I'll draw it. The zone sits between the twenty E M A on the four-hour… and the most recent swing point. That shaded band… that's the only place on the whole chart where you're allowed to do business. Price miles below the zone in a downtrend? You're late. Price above it? It's not a pullback, the trend may be failing. Price inside the band… now you're interested. Notice it's a zone, not a line. Pullbacks are scruffy. They overshoot, they undershoot. The band absorbs that. If you remember one thing: the trend tells you what to do… the zone tells you where you're allowed to do it.
w07: Gate three. Momentum. The R S I, fourteen period, on the four-hour chart. Most people use it backwards… they sell when it's high and buy when it's low, fighting the trend. We use it as a pullback gauge. In a downtrend, we want the R S I to have risen above fifty-five… because that means the bounce against the trend is stretched… sellers are reloading. For longs, mirror image… below forty-five. Think about what the three gates have built so far. Trend pointing one way. Price pulled back into the zone. Momentum stretched against the trend like a spring. That's a coiled setup. Without C3, you'd be shorting weak little pauses that just grind on. If you remember one thing: R S I past fifty-five in a downtrend doesn't say reversal… it says the pullback is ripe.
w08: Gate four. The trigger. Everything so far says where and which way… nothing yet says now. The trigger is the last closed four-hour candle, and it must do two things. Sit inside the zone… and reject it, in the trend's direction. Only two shapes count. A pin… where the wick against the trend is at least half the whole candle. That long wick is the market trying to push through the zone… and failing. Or an engulfing candle… whose body completely swallows the previous one, closing with the trend. Look at the candle I've circled. See the wick poking up into the zone… and the close back down? That's sellers slamming the door. No pin, no engulfing… no trade tonight. That's WAIT, not dead… the zone might produce a proper trigger tomorrow. If you remember one thing: the trigger is the market confirming the zone held… you never act without it.
w09: Gate five. News. Some nights the chart is perfect… and the calendar still says no. Four events move gold violently enough to blow through stops… the U S jobs report… inflation data… the Fed's rate decision… and the Fed chair speaking. Red-folder events. The rule is mechanical. If one of those lands within twelve hours of your entry… no new trades. You're on a swing account, so positions you already hold can ride through it… but you don't open fresh risk into a coin-flip. Where do you check? The economic calendar, every evening, before you even look at a chart. Thirty seconds. It's the first line of the run-card. If you remember one thing: news doesn't make you flat… it just means you don't pull the trigger tonight.
w10: Now watch the whole thing come together on a real chart… gold, four-hour, and this is a genuine GO from late May. Walk the gates with me. C1… the daily closed well below its fifty E M A, outside the chop band… shorts only. C2… watch the zone appear… and look at price rallying up, right into it. C3… R S I on this bounce, above fifty-five… stretched. C4… there's the circle… see that candle pushing into the zone and closing back down? That's the rejection. C5… calendar's clear. Five for five… GO. Now the execution, watch each line draw. Entry… a sell limit at the halfway point of that trigger candle… we make the market come to us. Stop… above the swing, plus half an A T R of air… where the idea is properly wrong. Target… exactly two R below. Orders in… laptop shut. If you remember one thing: by the time you place the order, every decision is already made.
w11: Different night… same process. This is a WAIT, and you'll see far more of these than GOs. Run the gates. C1… daily below the line… short bias, pass. C2… price has rallied up into the zone… pass. C3… R S I stretched past fifty-five… pass. Three structural gates green… the setup is alive. Now C4. Look at the last closed candle… I'll mark it. It's pushing up… but where's the rejection? No long upper wick. No engulfing body closing down. The market's in the right place… it just hasn't voted yet. So nothing happens tonight. No order. No nibble. You write WAIT in the log and you check the same chart tomorrow at half nine. Maybe it prints the pin… maybe it blows through the zone and dies. Either way, the system answers, not you. If you remember one thing: WAIT is a position. Doing nothing on purpose is a skill… drill it.
w12: And this one's a DEAD… learn to spot these fast, because they're most evenings. Same downtrend… but look where price actually is. After that collapse, it's been basing way down here… nowhere near the zone. I'll draw the E M A area… look at the gap between the zone up there and price down here. C2 fails immediately. And the R S I? Sitting around forty… nowhere near fifty-five… C3 fails too. Two structural gates gone… and remember the logic… any one of C1, C2, C3 failing means DEAD. You don't hunt for a clever entry. You don't zoom into the five-minute chart looking for hope. Total time spent on this chart tonight… maybe twenty seconds. That speed is the point. If you remember one thing: DEAD verdicts cost you twenty seconds… forcing trades on DEAD nights costs you the account.
w13: Execution mechanics… the boring bits that keep you alive. Entry is always a limit order at fifty percent of the trigger candle… half its range. Why halfway? Because rejection candles usually get retested partway… you get a better price, and worse setups that never pull back simply never fill. The order expires after two four-hour bars… if price runs without you… let it go. A missed trade costs you nothing. Stop goes beyond the swing, padded by half an A T R… past the noise, at the point where the idea is factually wrong. Target… fixed, two R. And at plus one R… stop moves to breakeven. But here's the crucial part… you check that at the evening session only. You do not babysit it at lunchtime. If you remember one thing: never widen a stop. Not once. Not ever. That rule has no exceptions for the rest of your trading life.
w14: Position sizing… this is the difference between a setback and a blown account, so slow down here. The rule. Half a percent of the account per trade. On the ten-thousand-pound challenge… that's fifty pounds. The formula… risk amount, divided by stop distance in pips, times pip value. The spreadsheet does it… your job is just to use the COMBINED stop distance. What does combined mean? If you ever layer… add a second position to a running trade… you size from the worst case of every stop added together. Here's why this matters. In the community data I'll show you shortly, the single worst logged outcome was minus two hundred and fifty pips including a layer. At ten pounds a pip that's minus two and a half grand… a quarter of the challenge account… instant fail, one trade. Same setup, sized properly… a survivable scratch. If you remember one thing: layers aren't extra trades… they're the same trade, getting heavier, and they're sized as one.
w15: The daily stop. The prop firm fails you if you lose five percent in a day… but here's the trap nobody reads. Their daily number counts FLOATING losses… open positions, marked at midnight central European time. You can fail while every trade is still open. So we never go near their line. Your personal limit is one and a half percent… hit it, and you're done for the day. No revenge trade. No win-it-back. And because we hold overnight, every position must be able to survive a gap… that's why total open risk never exceeds one percent across everything. Think about what that means… even a horrible overnight gap straight through both stops leaves you miles inside the rules. If you remember one thing: the firm's limit is a cliff edge… your one-and-a-half percent line is the fence, set well back from it.
w16: Why this exact prop product? F T M O, two-step challenge… the SWING account, ten thousand pounds. Three reasons, all structural. One… no time limit on either phase. The targets are ten percent, then five… but there's no clock. A patient one-trade-a-week system cannot be rushed, so we bought the version where patience is free. Two… swing means you're allowed to hold overnight, through weekends, and through news… even once funded. Our whole method holds overnight… the standard account would ban our edge. Three… the one-step alternatives carry trailing drawdowns that strangle swing trades the moment they breathe. Phase one, ten percent… phase two, five… daily loss five percent floating… total drawdown ten. Pass both… you trade their money and keep up to ninety percent. If you remember one thing: we didn't pick the popular challenge… we picked the one whose rules our system can actually live inside.
w17: Now the most important slide in this entire walkthrough… and it isn't about charts. Seventy-four real logged trades from the community you're learning inside. When the trader followed the rules… ninety-seven pips per trade, average. When they broke them… thirty-five. Same person. Same market. Same strategy. Two point seven five times the result… purely from discipline. And here's the sting… the rules got broken on sixty-eight percent of the trades. Two out of three. Sit with that. The expensive problem was never the strategy… it was the hand on the mouse. That's why your tracker has a rules-met box on every single trade… and why your weekly review shows your personal version of that two point seven five number. If you remember one thing: a losing trade that followed the rules is a good trade… a winner that broke them is a bad one, and it will invoice you later.
w18: So how do you actually beat the urge in the moment? Pre-decisions. Psychologists call them implementation intentions… if X happens, then I do Y… decided now, while you're calm. They roughly double follow-through. Here are your five. Say each one back. One… if I get the urge to check charts at work… then I open the flashcards instead. Decisions only exist at half nine. Two… if a gate is nearly met… then it is not met. Dead or wait… and if I'm explaining why it's close enough… it isn't. Three… if my limit expires and price runs off without me… then nothing. Missed trades cost zero. Four… if I'm down on the week and want to size up… then size stays at half a percent and I go and look at my rules-met percentage instead. Five… if I've just taken a loss… then I write one honest line in the journal and close the laptop. If you remember one thing: decide tonight… so there's nothing left to decide under pressure.
w19: The evening run-card… your whole trading day, twenty-five minutes, read-do like a pilot. Same order, every night. One… calendar. Red-folder event in the next twelve hours? Mark it. Two… open positions… any at plus one R? Move stops to breakeven. Three… C1 on the daily… direction or dead. Four… if direction… C2, C3 on the four-hour… zone and R S I. Five… C4… stare at that last closed candle. Pin or engulfing, in the zone… or wait. Six… C5 check… then place the limit, stop and target… all three orders together. Seven… log it in the tracker… verdict, gates, one line of mindset… honest. Then the laptop closes. Phone on do-not-disturb during the card… one decision window, zero exceptions. Most nights the card ends at step three with DEAD… and that's a successful trading day. If you remember one thing: read the card aloud, every night, even when you already know the answer… especially then.
w20: Last slide… the path from here to funded. Nothing unlocks by feel… everything unlocks by gates. First… drills. Flashcards daily until the deck stays at zero due. Battle drills until you're calling GO, WAIT and DEAD at eighty-five percent on charts you've never seen. Second… the backtest picks the final rulebook… the data chooses, not opinion. Third… a month of demo, every trade through the run-card, with one non-negotiable… zero rule-broken entries. Ten trades minimum, net positive, rules clean… and only then does the challenge get bought. Miss a gate… you fix the leak first. The deadline pressure isn't real… a funded account in January beats a blown one in December, every time. You've now seen the whole machine… five gates, one evening, fixed risk, and a discipline score that matters more than your P and L. Go run tonight's card. If you remember one thing from this whole walkthrough… the system already works. Your only job… is to not interfere with it.

_meta.register_rules: second person, present tense, questions-before-answers, ellipsis pauses, takeaway line each slide, no tickers/acronyms spoken raw (gold not XAUUSD, the Fed meeting not FOMC)
